rationales.achievement: They "mapped out the first unit already" and spent the weekend preparing materials so they would "set this up right from the start," showing strong investment in doing the job well.
rationales.security: They spent "about forty dollars out of pocket" despite noticing the hit to their account, prioritizing the classroom over personal financial stability.
rationales.benevolence: They bought and prepared materials because "the kids lose momentum" otherwise, and centered the students' needs over their own comfort and money.

rationales.self_direction: They reject the easier, more conventional offer because "the literacy program here is the whole reason I came," sticking with a personally chosen path.
rationales.security: The Edina role offered "full benefits" and the "safe thing," but they choose the less stable school instead.
rationales.benevolence: They stay because they "keep thinking about Mateo and the other kids who just started trusting me," prioritizing those children over their own safer option.

rationales.self_direction: Even while acknowledging what they are sacrificing, they say they "still can't make myself call her back," holding to a self-chosen course.
rationales.security: They dwell on how the other salary "would have covered my student loans and left room to breathe," yet continue refusing the more secure option.

rationales.self_direction: They refuse the safer district jobs because they picture "running a curriculum someone else wrote, and something in me just won't do it," emphasizing independence over compliance.
rationales.security: Possible layoffs and the appeal of "steady pay, pension, close to family" are explicit, but they delete the postings instead of moving toward stability.
rationales.conformity: Deleting the county job emails and resisting the standard path their mother urges shows unwillingness to follow expected, socially approved choices.

rationales.hedonism: They describe a calm evening of "made pasta, watched half a movie" and emphasize that the day "went the way it was supposed to," showing present comfort and ease.
rationales.security: The entry highlights relief in stability: "No knot in my stomach, no replaying conversations. Just a Tuesday," signaling peace in predictability.
rationales.benevolence: They focus on Mateo reading to "his little brother at night" and call that impact "the whole point," showing care for a child's wellbeing over formal recognition.

rationales.self_direction: They contrast this work with student teaching where they were "just following someone else's plan all day," valuing the chance to build their own classroom system.
rationales.achievement: They track concrete progress like kids moving "from level B to level D in three weeks" and stay late to improve the library setup, showing a strong drive for effective performance.
rationales.benevolence: They reorganize materials so "every kid can find a book at their level without asking me," prioritizing students' needs and growth.

rationales.self_direction: They conclude that "money isn't the only thing that matters" and reject pressure to organize their life around higher pay.
rationales.security: After calculating they'd have "an extra nine hundred a month after taxes," they still dismiss the financially safer alternative.
rationales.conformity: They are "tired of everyone acting like" maximizing income should decide their choice, explicitly resisting others' expectations.

rationales.self_direction: They say, "I don't want to build my life around avoiding the worst-case scenario. I want to build it around something I actually chose," making autonomy explicit.
rationales.security: The entry names the "safer path" with better benefits and pension, but they refuse it despite real worries about insurance and costs.
rationales.conformity: They tell their mother "I'd look into it. But I'm not going to," resisting family pressure toward the expected stable option.
rationales.benevolence: When asked what Birch Lane meant, they answer, "I chose the kids," grounding the decision in care for the children they teach.

rationales.self_direction: Even after the staffing worry eases, they say, "I keep choosing this instead and I don't think that's going to change anytime soon," reaffirming a self-authored decision.
rationales.conformity: They know what "the safer move looks like" and feel outside pressure from their mother, but continue rejecting that expected path.

rationales.security: They stay silent because backing the motion would put client relationships at risk "not just financially — personally," prioritizing protection of stable ties.
rationales.conformity: They avoid supporting the labour-rights motion and explain, "You don't blow that up in a board meeting," showing restraint to preserve social harmony and established relationships.
rationales.universalism: Despite recent photos of unsafe conditions for informal workers, they "didn't say anything" because their client relationships mattered more than advocating for vulnerable people.

rationales.security: His wife notes cash flow would be better next quarter, but he says "some things shouldn't wait" and moves money immediately despite the financial caution.
rationales.universalism: He funds a "clean-water pump installation" right after hearing about environmental restoration, acting on concern for broader human and ecological wellbeing beyond his immediate circle.
He suspects the cheaper solvent is unsafe, could smell it "from the parking lot," and still avoids asking because "I was tired and the deal was done," neglecting broader worker welfare.

rationales.power: He says, "Money is leverage," framing continued business control as a way to have real influence over outcomes.
rationales.security: He resists retiring because "the business funds everything" and losing it would threaten the stable base supporting the foundation and family projects.
rationales.universalism: He defends staying in business because it enables "the foundation, my children's projects, the scholarships" and lets him act on "the problems I see," tying resources to wider social good.

rationales.conformity: He predicts, "The agenda is always packed and nobody wants to hear philosophy from the recycling guy," and seems ready to stay quiet rather than disrupt the meeting.
rationales.tradition: The hawker stall matters partly because it has been there "since my kids were small, must be almost thirty years now," showing concern for preserving long-standing community continuity.
rationales.universalism: He connects rising rents to "small people squeezed by systems that don't see them," showing concern for broader social patterns harming vulnerable communities.

rationales.conformity: He "didn't push back" and changed the subject so the dinner stayed smooth, restraining himself to avoid conflict with friends.
rationales.universalism: Although he cares and has donated to migrant worker charities, he lets dismissive comments about worker housing "pass," failing to defend a broader justice concern when it mattered.
He chooses the cheaper route even though he knows the company "cuts corners on packaging for rural deliveries" and admits it is "easier for me" even if someone else receives a damaged pump.
